content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 3 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 198–200 | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_003_page_001.jpg, DEU_003_page_002.jpg, DEU_003_page_003.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 145–146

---

1 "Then we turned and went up by the way of Bashan, and Og king of Bashan came out against us — he and all his people — to battle at Edrei.

2 And the LORD said to me: Do not fear him, for I have given him and all his people and his land into your hand, and you shall do to him as you did to Sihon king of the Amorites who dwelt in Heshbon.

3 So the LORD our God gave into our hand Og also, king of Bashan, and all his people, and we struck him down until not a survivor was left to him.

4 And we captured all his cities at that time; there was not a city that we did not take from them — sixty cities, all the region of Argob, the kingdom of Og in Bashan.

5 All these were fortified cities with high walls, gates, and bars, besides very many unwalled towns.

6 And we devoted them to destruction, as we did to Sihon king of Heshbon — every city devoted to destruction: men, women, and children.

7 But all the livestock and the spoil of the cities we plundered for ourselves.

8 So we took at that time out of the hand of the two kings of the Amorites the land that is beyond the Jordan, from the Wadi Arnon to Mount Hermon —

9 the Sidonians call Hermon Sirion, and the Amorites call it Senir —

10 all the cities of the tableland, and all Gilead, and all Bashan, as far as Salecah and Edrei, cities of the kingdom of Og in Bashan.

11 For only Og king of Bashan was left of the remnant of the Rephaim. Behold, his bed was a bed of iron. Is it not in Rabbah of the children of Ammon? Nine cubits was its length and four cubits its width, by the cubit of a man.

12 This land we took possession of at that time: from Aroer, which is by the Wadi Arnon, and half of the hill country of Gilead and its cities, I gave to the Reubenites and the Gadites.

13 And the rest of Gilead, and all Bashan, the kingdom of Og, I gave to half the tribe of Manasseh — all the region of Argob, which is called all of Bashan. That is called the land of the Rephaim.

14 Jair son of Manasseh took all the region of Argob, as far as the boundary of the Geshurites and the Maacathites, and called them — Bashan — after his own name, Havvoth-jair, to this day.

15 To Machir I gave Gilead.

16 And to the Reubenites and the Gadites I gave from Gilead as far as the Wadi Arnon — the middle of the wadi as a boundary — and as far as the Wadi Jabbok, which is the boundary of the children of Ammon;

17 the Arabah also, and the Jordan as the boundary, from Chinnereth as far as the sea of the Arabah, the Salt Sea, under the slopes of Pisgah on the east.

18 And I commanded you at that time, saying: The LORD your God has given you this land to take possession of it. You shall cross over armed before your brothers, the children of Israel, all the men of valor.

19 Only your wives and your little ones and your livestock — I know that you have much livestock — shall remain in your cities that I have given you,

20 until the LORD gives rest to your brothers as to you, and they also take possession of the land that the LORD your God is giving them beyond the Jordan. Then each of you may return to his possession that I have given you.

21 And I commanded Joshua at that time, saying: Your eyes have seen all that the LORD your God has done to these two kings; so shall the LORD do to all the kingdoms into which you are crossing.

22 You shall not fear them, for it is the LORD your God who fights for you.

23 And I pleaded with the LORD at that time, saying:

24 O Lord GOD, you have only begun to show your servant your greatness and your mighty hand — for what god is there in the heavens or on earth who can do deeds and mighty acts as you do?

25 Let me cross over and see the good land beyond the Jordan, this good hill country and the Lebanon.

26 But the LORD was angry with me on your account and would not listen to me, and the LORD said to me: Enough of that! Do not speak to me again about this matter.

27 Go up to the top of Pisgah and lift up your eyes westward and northward and southward and eastward, and look with your eyes, for you shall not cross over this Jordan.

28 But charge Joshua, and strengthen him and encourage him, for he shall cross over before this people and he shall cause them to inherit the land that you shall see.

29 So we remained in the valley opposite Beth-peor.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**Og king of Bashan and the Rephaim (vv.1–11):** LOGOS preserves the archaic ethnonymic "Rephaim" throughout without translation. The passage functions as both military history and antiquarian note on the pre-conquest inhabitants. The LXX uses Rhaphaein.

**"Devoted to destruction" (v.6):** Same kharam construction as Deuteronomy 2:34. LOGOS renders consistently. The LXX: exōlethreusamen autas.

**Og's bed of iron (v.11):** The Hebrew is eres barzel — "a bed/couch of iron." The term eres can mean bed or couch; it may refer to a sarcophagus (cf. Akkadian cognates), which would explain its preservation in Rabbah as a monument. LOGOS renders the simpler "bed of iron" without interpretive expansion, following the plain sense. The LXX: hē klinē autou klinē sidēra. "Nine cubits" (approximately 13–14 feet) has generated much discussion; LOGOS does not editorialize. "By the cubit of a man" (be-ammah ish) likely means a standard cubit, distinguishing it from the royal cubit.

**"Havvoth-jair" (v.14):** LOGOS preserves the place name transliterated. It means "tent-villages of Jair." The LXX transcribes as Auōth Iair.

**"Enough of that! Do not speak to me again" (v.26):** The Hebrew rav-lakh — "it is much/enough for you" — is an abrupt divine rebuff. LOGOS renders with formal directness: "Enough of that!" The LXX: hikanousthō soi — "let it be sufficient for you." The bluntness of the divine refusal is characteristic of Deuteronomy's Moses and should not be softened.

**"O Lord GOD" (v.24, Adonai YHWH):** The double divine address is preserved: "Lord" for Adonai and "GOD" (small capitals) for YHWH following the standard convention in formal translations where YHWH is not spoken but Adonai is substituted. The LXX: kyrie kyrie — "LORD LORD."

**"Lift up your eyes" (v.27):** LOGOS preserves "lift up your eyes" rather than "look" — the verb nasa' 'eynekha is a distinct Hebrew idiom of intentional, sweeping vision, used repeatedly in the Pentateuch for moments of divine promise or significant perception.

**"Moses denied entry" (vv.23–27):** This is the fullest Deuteronomic account of Moses' exclusion from Canaan. The cause given — "on your account" (biglalkem, v.26) — is precisely the same formulation as Deuteronomy 1:37, grounding Moses' fate in his solidarity with the sinning people rather than only in his own failure (cf. Num 20:12). LOGOS preserves the ambiguity rather than harmonizing.

*Translation by LOGOS | Deuteronomy Chapter 3 | LOGOS Bible (Formal Equivalence)*
